The Real — Ibn Arabi's central divine name. Reality itself is the divine self-disclosure. All existence is Al-Haqq making itself known through its own attributes. The cosmos is the dream of God knowing itself.

The First Intellect emanating from the One — Al-Farabi's Islamic Neoplatonism maps Plotinus's Nous onto the Islamic divine order. The intellect of the First Intellect generates the ten intellects of the planetary spheres.
Founder of Shugendo — the Japanese mountain asceticism that combines Shinto, Buddhist, and Taoist elements. Said to command demons and fly through the air.
The Avatar Buddhas of the mountains — in shinbutsu-shugo (Shinto-Buddhist syncretism), Japanese mountain kami are avatars (gongen) of specific Buddhas. Zao Gongen is the most famous.
The Mordvin/Erzya supreme deity — the great god (Ine-Pas = Great God). Creator of the world. The Mordvin pantheon includes Sky God, Earth Mother, Water Mother, and a host of lesser pas (gods).

The lwa of love, luxury, and feminine power — the Haitian Venus. She loves beautiful things, weeps for humanity's suffering and her own unfulfillment, and wears three wedding rings (for three husbands: Damballa, Ogou, Agwe).

The great snake lwa — the oldest and most venerable. He is the cosmic serpent, the rainbow serpent, the creator who lives in trees and springs. White, pure, ancient beyond words. He and his wife Ayida Wedo are the serpent couple that supports the sky.
The warrior lwa of iron and revolution — the Haitian transformation of Yoruba Ogun. He carries a machete and wears red. Patron of soldiers and revolution; the spirit of Haitian independence.
The cosmic anaconda of Shipibo-Conibo cosmology — the great serpent whose body generates the geometric patterns (kene) that structure reality. Shamanic (ayahuasca) contact reveals her patterns.
The spirit of the Jurema tree (Mimosa hostilis) — the sacred plant deity of the Brazilian Jurema religion. The tree contains the divine drink that allows shamanic journeys.
The Santeria transformation of Yoruba Orunmila — master of Ifa divination. He knows every person's destiny because he witnessed creation. Syncretically identified with Saint Francis of Assisi.
The Santeria Obatala — creator of human bodies, lord of white and purity. Syncretically identified with Our Lady of Mercy (Las Mercedes). The supreme creative principle in Lucumi theology.
Sister of Isis and Osiris — the shadow twin of Isis. While Isis represents the visible, Nephthys represents the hidden. She mourns with Isis at Osiris's death. Lady of the House.
The crocodile god — lord of the Nile, fertility, and military power. Associated with the pharaoh's might. His major temple at Kom Ombo shares space with Horus the Elder.
The ram-headed potter god — shapes human bodies and their ka (spiritual double) on his potter's wheel. His temple at Elephantine, at the source of the Nile.
Falcon-headed war god of Thebes — the original patron of the pharaoh's military might before Amun rose to prominence. His fierce power was the epitome of royal military valor.
Moon goddess — the moon itself, driving her chariot across the night sky. She fell in love with Endymion and put him in eternal sleep so she could kiss him each night.
The sun itself — drives his chariot across the sky each day. His son Phaethon borrowed it, lost control, and Zeus had to destroy him with a thunderbolt to save the world.
Goddess of victory — stands beside Zeus, carries a palm branch and wreath. Her winged form was one of the most reproduced images in the ancient world.

God of medicine and healing — son of Apollo, killed by Zeus for raising the dead. His snake-staff (Rod of Asclepius) is still the symbol of medicine. His temples (Asclepia) combined healing, dream-incubation, and religious practice.
The goddess of dawn — the rosy-fingered dawn who opens the gates of heaven for the sun. The most directly surviving PIE *h2ewsos in Greek. She loved mortal men (Tithonus) who aged while she did not.
Necessity — the primordial goddess of inevitability, compulsion, and fate. Even the Moirai are instruments of Ananke. Plato's Spindle of Ananke holds the eight spheres of the cosmos.
Queen of the Roman gods — guardian of the state, women, and marriage. Her anger at Troy persisted through the Aeneid. The Capitoline Triad: Jupiter, Juno, Minerva.
Goddess of wisdom, crafts, and strategic warfare — uniquely Roman; born from Jupiter's head. Patron of teachers, artisans, and doctors. Part of the Capitoline Triad.

deities.earliest_attestation: Republican period; mystery cult from c. 200 BCE
deities.summary: The Roman Dionysus — god of wine and ecstatic release. His mystery cult (Bacchanalia) was suppressed by the Senate in 186 BCE for alleged immorality, then rehabilitated. His mysteries promised afterlife.
The Roman lord of the underworld — Hades in his wealth aspect (Pluto = the wealthy one, for underground riches). His cult was more positive than the Greek Hades — he was a generous patron.
God of the sea and fresh water — not exactly Poseidon's Roman equivalent; he had domain over all water sources, not just the sea. Horse deity as well (Equestrian Neptune).
God of merchants, travelers, communication, and the underworld — Roman Hermes. The most syncretically absorbed deity in the Roman Empire (identified with Celtic Lugus, Germanic Wotan, etc.).
Roman goddess of grain and harvest — her name gives us cereal. Mother of Proserpina. The Aventine triad (Ceres, Liber, Libera) was the plebeian parallel to the Capitoline triad.

The Gorgon whose gaze turns to stone — once a beautiful priestess of Athena, transformed as punishment. Killed by Perseus. Her blood generated Pegasus and the origin of snakes in Libya. Her severed head became Athena's shield.
The identity of the individual soul (Atman) with the cosmic soul (Brahman) — "Tat tvam asi" (That art thou). The central insight of Vedanta.
The emptiness of inherent existence — all phenomena lack a fixed, independent self. Nagarjuna's Madhyamaka philosophy made this the foundation of Mahayana.
The teaching, the truth, the cosmic law — in Buddhism, the second of the Three Jewels. The Buddha's teaching as a living cosmic principle.
The Spirit of God that hovers over the primordial waters before creation — the divine breath/wind as cosmic principle.
The great prophet who was taken to heaven in a fiery chariot without dying — expected to return before the Day of the LORD.
The household gods of Rome — Lares are ancestral protective spirits, Penates are guardians of the pantry. Every Roman household maintained their domestic cult.

The ten-headed demon-king who abducted Sita — the great antagonist of the Ramayana. Also the greatest devotee of Shiva, whose hymn (Shiva Tandava Stotra) he composed. The most theologically complex adversary in Indian religion.
The Buddhist tempter — tempted Siddhartha the night of his enlightenment with desire (daughters), fear (army), and doubt. Lord of the realm of samsara that keeps beings trapped.
The PIE cosmic truth/order — Avestan Asha (opposed by Druj/Lie), Sanskrit Rita (cosmic order). The moral order built into the structure of reality.
Goddess of sin and purification — she eats filth (sexual transgression) from dying confessors. Both causes and cleanses erotic sin.
Seven Serpent — Aztec goddess of corn and sustenance. Seven represents harvest abundance.
She of the Jade Skirt — goddess of rivers and storms, wife of Tlaloc. Destroyed the fourth world with a great flood.
Snake Woman — earth mother and war goddess. She appears carrying an empty cradle, wailing — an omen of war and sacrifice.
Cloud Serpent — god of the hunt and the Milky Way. Father of Quetzalcoatl in some Toltec traditions.
The corn god — the male divine corn, paired with Chicomecóatl. Son of Tlazolteotl in some accounts.
God of death and ruler of Metnal (the lowest underworld) — depicted as a skeletal figure wearing bells. The most feared Mayan deity.
The younger Hero Twin — becomes the moon after defeating the lords of Xibalba alongside his brother Hunahpu.
The elder Hero Twin — becomes the sun after victory in the underworld. Father of the Maize God.
The Earthquake Maker — son of Seven Macaw, boasted he could shake mountains. Defeated by the Hero Twins.
Thor's wife — her golden hair was cut off by Loki, then replaced by the dwarves with magical gold. Possibly an earth goddess whose hair is the grain.
The Silent God — son of Odin, second strongest of all gods. Will survive Ragnarok and avenge Odin by killing Fenrir.
Born for revenge — Odin begot him specifically to avenge Baldur's death. Killed Hod the day after his birth. Survives Ragnarok.
God of poetry and eloquence — the skald of the gods, husband of Idun. He who greets the slain at Valhalla.
Son of Baldur — god of justice and reconciliation. The best judge among gods and men, settles all disputes.
God of skiing, hunting, and single combat — invoked on oaths alongside Odin. One of the most ancient Norse deities.
Guardian of the Well of Wisdom at Yggdrasil's root — Odin sacrificed his eye to drink from it. After Mimir's death, Odin preserved his head and consulted it for counsel.
The wisest being ever — created from the saliva of all gods. Killed by dwarves who brewed the Mead of Poetry from his blood.
Sea goddess who collects the drowned in her net — wife of Aegir, mother of the nine wave-daughters. To drown is to be taken by Ran.
Lord of the sea — brews ale for the gods in an enormous cauldron. His nine daughters are the waves.
The goddess of righteous retribution — punishes hubris and undeserved good fortune. The cosmic balancer of excess.
Goddess of fortune and luck — spins the wheel of fate, her gifts are unpredictable. Roman equivalent: Fortuna.
God of the wild, shepherds, and rustic music — half-man, half-goat, son of Hermes. His sudden appearance caused panic.
Goddess of the rainbow — divine messenger before Hermes took that role, connecting heaven and earth with her rainbow bridge.
Personification of gentle death — twin of Hypnos (Sleep), son of Nyx. The process of dying as divine presence.
God of sleep — twin of Thanatos, lives in the underworld where sun never shines. Even Zeus feared Hypnos.
The shape-shifting sea god who knows all things — must be caught and held through all his transformations to force him to tell the truth.
The deified Romulus — third member of the archaic Roman triad (Jupiter, Mars, Quirinus). The founder of Rome made divine.
Goddess of fruit trees and orchards — uniquely Roman with no Greek equivalent. Won by Vertumnus after his many disguises.
God of seasons, change, and gardens — shapeshifted through many forms to win Pomona's love.
Goddess of flowers and spring — her festival Floralia (April 28) was a raucous celebration of spring's return.
Roman lord of the underworld and underground wealth — equated with Pluto/Hades. Connected to the Gauls through Caesar.
God of fertility and the eastern desert — depicted ithyphallically, associated with lettuce (considered aphrodisiac). One of the oldest Egyptian deities.
Cat goddess — originally lioness, later domesticated into the gentle cat. Goddess of home, cats, music, and dancing. Her annual festival involved enormous numbers of pilgrims.
Lord of the Memphite necropolis — god of the funerary realm specifically at Memphis. Later merged with Osiris and Ptah as Ptah-Sokar-Osiris.
She Who Loves Silence — the cobra goddess of the Valley of the Kings, protecting the royal tombs.
Cobra goddess of harvest and nourishment — she decided each person's ren (name and fate) at birth. Lady of the granary.
Wife of the Moon God Nanna — mother of Inanna and Utu/Shamash. She pleaded with the divine assembly to spare Ur from destruction; they refused.
primary_sources: Lament for the Destruction of Ur

deities.summary: Goddess of grain, writing, and scribal arts — the original Sumerian divine scribe, patron of all scribes before Nabu took the role.
God of war and agriculture — slays the cosmic serpent Asag and the Anzu bird. The archetype of the champion-hero dragon-slayer in Mesopotamia.
King of the wind-demons — terrifying in appearance but used apotropaically against Lamashtu. His image on amulets warded off the child-killing demon.
Sumerian storm god — the Sumerian parallel of Akkadian Adad. Lord of rain and thunder, son of Enlil.
The divine horsemen twins — physicians of the gods, they rescue the drowning, restore youth, bring light at dawn. The Vedic parallel of the Dioscuri.
The storm troop of Indra — 27 or 49 shining warriors who ride with Indra in battle, wielding lightning. Sons of Rudra.
The howling storm god — dangerous, wild, both the source of disease and its healer. Father of the Maruts. Shiva's direct predecessor in the Vedic tradition.
Lord of wealth and king of the Yakshas — guardian of the north quarter and keeper of all divine treasure. Lives on Mount Kailash near Shiva.
God of erotic love — shoots five flower-tipped arrows that cause specific types of longing. Burned to ash by Shiva's third eye; revived through Parvati's love.
Goddess of the night — sister of Ushas (Dawn). One of the most beautiful hymns in the Rigveda addresses her: she illuminates the night with stars.
The divine craftsman of the gods — fashioned the vajra for Indra, made the first soma cup. The divine smith who shapes the implements of cosmic order.
The divine architect — built Lanka for Ravana, the city of Dwaraka for Krishna, and the vehicles and weapons of all the devas.
The Immovable King — wrathful protector of practice, cannot be moved from truth. Blue, with sword and lasso. Most important of the Five Wisdom Kings in Japanese Shingon Buddhism.
The enlightened layman — the bodhisattva in ordinary life. His thunderous silence in response to Manjushri's question about non-duality is the most celebrated moment in Mahayana literature.
The swift-acting Tara — her right foot is extended, ready to spring into action. Faster to answer prayers than any other deity. Specifically for protection from eight fears.
The People of the Goddess Danu — the divine race of Ireland who defeated the Fomorians, were then defeated by the Milesians, and retreated into the sidhe (fairy mounds).
The Silver Wheel — daughter of Don, mother of the twins Lleu and Dylan (the sons she tried to deny). Her name means "silver wheel" — a moon and fate goddess.
The patient craftsman — Welsh equivalent of Manannán Mac Lir. After losing his kingdom, mastered three crafts to sustain his companions.
The Slavic sky-smith — the divine fire of heaven, smith of the gods. Father of Dazbog (the sun) and Svarozhich (the hearth fire).
The Slavic sun god — giver of wealth and prosperity, son of Svarog. The Slavic solar deity.
The midsummer deity — bonfires, water, ferns, and fertility. His festival (Ivan Kupala) is one of the most widely preserved Slavic pre-Christian celebrations.
Goddess of winter and death — her effigy is drowned or burned at the end of winter to welcome spring and the green god Jarilo.
Gaulish thunder god — the wheel is his sacred symbol. Lucan lists him among the three Gaulish gods who received human sacrifice.
Tribal deity — "god of the tribe/people." Equated by Romans with Mars or Mercury. Required sacrifice by drowning in a cauldron.
The Good Striker — mallet god of the Gauls. His mallet could both kill and revive. Associated with forests and Celtic underworld.
God of war and humanity — the red fierce deity who refused to hide from the primal parents' children. Humans are his food.
God of cultivated food and peace — Rongo and Tu are the twin poles of Maori theology: peace and war.
The moon goddess of Polynesia — weaves tapa cloth on the moon, governs lunar cycles, associated with women's crafts.
The good creator of Vanuatu mythology — opposed by the evil Suqe. Together they made the imperfect world.
The Samoan supreme being — Tagaloa created the sky, then the earth, then sent birds to see if the earth was ready.
The great mountain kami — father of Konohanasakuya-hime (the ancestor of emperors). Lord of all mountains.
Princess of Blossoming Trees — wife of Ninigi (Amaterasu's grandson), divine ancestor of the Japanese imperial line. She proved her fidelity by giving birth in a burning hut.
God of thunder and lightning — depicted as a fearsome figure beating drums made of clouds. Twin of Fujin.
God of wind — carries a large bag of winds. Twin of Raijin. Together they guard the gates of shrines.
Dragon King of the Sea — rules the ocean from his undersea palace Ryugu-jo, controls the tides with magical tide jewels.
The divine ghost-catcher — a scholar who failed the imperial exams and killed himself; appointed King of the Ghosts by the Jade Emperor to compensate.
The Earth God — the most local of all deities. Every neighborhood, field, and building has its own Tu Di Gong who reports annually to the Jade Emperor.
The Kitchen God — watches the family all year and reports to the Jade Emperor before the New Year. His picture is burned to send him to heaven.
Duke of Thunder — strikes those who commit hidden crimes that escape earthly justice. His weapon exposes evil.
Mother of Lightning — the female counterpart of Lei Gong, uses mirrors to create the flashes of lightning that illuminate evil.
The Two Door Gods — divine generals who guard the doors of homes and temples against evil spirits. Based on generals of Tang Emperor Taizong.
Judge Bao — the historical incorruptible Song dynasty judge deified as a divine arbiter, accessible to common people seeking justice.

The Korean Maitreya — the future Buddha who will establish a perfect world. In a primordial contest, Mireuk lost the present world to Shakyamuni through Shakyamuni's trickery, explaining the world's imperfection.
The Birth Grandmother — triple goddess who presides over childbirth, child-rearing, and early childhood. Approached with prayer and rice offerings for health and fertility.
The lwa of the sea — admiral and master of the ocean, husband of Erzulie Freda. He accepts offerings of tiny boats sent out to sea.
Lwa of fresh water, crossroads, and magic — patron of sorcerers and diviners. He lives at the boundary of fresh and salt water.
The first mambo (Vodou priestess) — Ayizan initiates all Vodou priests and priestesses, presides over the beginnings of sacred ceremony.
The great trickster of the American Southwest — steals fire, disrupts creation, teaches through chaos and failure. Simultaneously creator and destroyer.
primary_sources: Southwest oral traditions (Navajo, Apache, Paiute, Shoshone)
alternate_names: Old Man Coyote (separate figure in Crow)

deities.summary: Thought Woman (Kokyangwuti) — she thinks things and they come into being. The Hopi first creator who thought the world into existence and helps those in trouble.
The divine mother of corn — the sustainer of life across the Eastern Woodlands. She sacrificed herself so the people could eat.
Mother Earth — Maka is the earth herself. All life comes from her and returns to her womb at death.
The Sun — Grandfather Wi, source of all light, warmth, and life. His strength and endurance are the divine qualities the Lakota admire.
Chief of the Thunderbirds — commands the storms that battle the water serpents (Horned Serpents) lurking in the deep. The cosmic conflict between sky and water.
The prophet-apostle of light — claimed to be the Paraclete promised by Jesus and the successor of Zoroaster, Buddha, and Christ. Executed by the Zoroastrian establishment.
The divine being who descended to fight Darkness — partially defeated, his scattered light-elements became the particles of divine light trapped in matter that constitute human souls.
The Lord of the World in Theosophical teaching — an Ancient of Days who has guided Earth's spiritual evolution for 18.5 million years from his retreat at Shambhala.
primary_sources: Alice Bailey, A Treatise on Cosmic Fire

deities.summary: The great Kushan mother goddess — a synthesis of Anahita, Mesopotamian Nana, and local Central Asian goddesses, widely worshipped from Bactria to Tang China on Silk Road routes.
God of forests and uncultivated land — the liminal deity of the boundary between civilized and wild. Worshipped by slaves and freedmen.

The Great Mother officially adopted by Rome in 204 BCE to win the Second Punic War. Her ecstatic cult with castrated priests (Galli) was the most foreign element ever officially incorporated into Roman religion.
Goddess of Night — mother of Sleep, Death, the Fates, Strife. Even Zeus feared her.
The primordial sky — castrated by Kronos on Gaia's instruction. Aphrodite born from sea foam around his severed genitals.
God of fire and the forge — the Roman Hephaestus, divine blacksmith.
One-handed god of oaths — sacrificed his hand binding Fenrir.
The Irish sea god — his four children were transformed into swans for 900 years by their jealous stepmother.
primary_sources: The Fate of the Children of Lir

deities.summary: Prince of Dyfed who traded places with Arawn, lord of the Otherworld for a year. The mortal who becomes divine through otherworldly encounter.
The Fon trickster-messenger — the youngest of the vodun, who speaks all languages and opens all roads.
The Bounteous/Holy Spirit — the divine emanation through which Ahura Mazda acts in the world. The spirit of good.
Good Mind — the first and greatest Amesha Spenta. He received the Gathas from Zarathustra.
Infinite Light — the three negatives of Kabbalistic theology: Ain (Nothing), Ain Soph (Infinite Nothing), Ain Soph Aur (Infinite Light).
The cosmic breath/consciousness — Sila is the air, the weather, and the intelligence permeating all things.
primary_sources: Inuit oral tradition (Rasmussen, Intellectual Culture of the Iglulik Eskimos)

deities.summary: The third being from Chaos — cosmic attraction that enables creation.
The final monster who challenged Zeus for the cosmos.
God of boundaries and property limits — even Jupiter must yield to him.
The Roman Kronos — god of the Golden Age.
Goddess of abundance and harvest — wife of Saturn.
Goddess of prophecy and childbirth.
Goddess of doorposts and hinges — Janus gave her power to repel evil from thresholds.
The self-created — the first to emerge from Nun.
The scarab who pushes the morning sun.
Akkadian storm god — the Akkadian Ishkur.
Grain god of the Philistines and Canaanites — father of Baal in some traditions.
Lord of beginnings — elephant head, remover of obstacles.
War god of youthful divine power, commander of the divine army.
Lord of death in Buddhist iconography — the judge of karma.
The trickster demigod who fished up islands and slowed the sun.
The trickster who speaks all languages and must be propitiated first.
First-born of Kronos and Rhea — the eldest Olympian who tends the eternal hearth flame. She gave up her throne for Dionysus to avoid conflict.
Goddess of strife — threw the Apple of Discord that caused the Trojan War. In Hesiod, she has two forms: the evil strife (war) and the good strife (competition that drives excellence).
God of dreams — takes human forms within dreams. Son of Hypnos, brother of Thanatos. He who shapes the dream.
Triple goddess of magic, crossroads, and the moon.

deities.earliest_attestation: Rabbinic period; developed in Kabbalah (Zohar, 13th c.)
deities.summary: The indwelling divine presence — the immanent, feminine aspect of God in Jewish tradition. In Kabbalah, the Shekhinah is the tenth Sefirah (Malkhut), the divine presence that dwells with Israel, goes into exile with the people, and is reunited with the transcendent masculine divine through righteous action.

facets.facet_name: The Unmanifest Source
facets.core_claim: The Monad is beyond name, form, gender, and category. It does not create directly; it self-contemplates, and from that contemplation the Aeons emerge. It is the silence before the first word.

facets.facet_name: The Demiurge (craftsman of matter)
facets.core_claim: Yaldabaoth, hidden away in a cloud by Sophia, fashions the material cosmos in imitation of the Pleroma he has never seen. He populates it with seven Archons and rules as if he were alone.

facets.facet_name: The False Most High (Lucifer-as-usurper)
facets.core_claim: Declares 'I am God and there is no other.' Rebuked from above: 'You are wrong, Samael — blind god.' This is the figure mainstream religion has mistaken for the Most High.

facets.facet_name: Creator of the heavens and the earth
facets.core_claim: In Genesis 1, Yahweh-Elohim fashions the cosmos from formless void. In orthodox reading, this is the ultimate God creating from nothing. In Gnostic reading, it is the Demiurge ordering pre-existing chaos in ignorance of the Pleroma.

facets.facet_name: The Jealous God of Sinai
facets.core_claim: 'I am the Lord thy God; thou shalt have no other gods before me. For I the Lord thy God am a jealous God.' (Exodus 20). Demands exclusive worship, blood sacrifice, and unflinching obedience.

facets.facet_name: The Covenantal Lord
facets.core_claim: Yahweh enters into covenant with Abraham, Isaac, Jacob; gives Torah to Moses; binds himself to Israel as their God.

facets.facet_name: Head of the Divine Council
facets.core_claim: El Elyon presides over the divine council and apportions the nations among the sons of God (Deut 32:8-9 in the older textual tradition). He is the true Most High in the Gnostic and older-Canaanite reading.

facets.facet_name: Protector of Ra's solar barque
facets.core_claim: Each night Set stands at the prow of Ra's barque and spears Apophis, the chaos-serpent. He is necessary chaos in service of cosmic order.

facets.facet_name: Murderer of Osiris, usurper of the throne
facets.core_claim: Set murders his brother Osiris out of jealousy, dismembers him, claims the throne. Embodies disorder and the violation of ma'at.

facets.facet_name: Lord of storms and the red desert
facets.core_claim: Set rules the red lands beyond the cultivated Nile valley — the desert, foreign nations, the wild places.

facets.facet_name: Cosmic orderer (slayer of Tiamat)
facets.core_claim: Marduk slays Tiamat, the primordial chaos-mother, splits her body to form heaven and earth, and is exalted by the gods with fifty names.

facets.facet_name: Champion against chaos
facets.core_claim: Marduk takes up arms when no other god will face Tiamat. Armed with thunderbolt and the four winds, he defeats her.

facets.facet_name: Goddess of love and fertility
facets.core_claim: Inanna is the bride of Dumuzi, the principle of erotic and fertile love that drives the renewal of the world.

facets.facet_name: Goddess of war
facets.core_claim: Inanna rides into battle on lions; her hymns celebrate her as the destroyer of cities and the breaker of armies.

facets.facet_name: The Descended One
facets.core_claim: Inanna descends through seven gates of the underworld, stripped at each gate of a divine attribute, until she stands naked before Ereshkigal. Killed and hung on a hook; revived by Enki's intervention; returns transformed.

facets.facet_name: Queen of Heaven
facets.core_claim: Inanna is the Queen of Heaven, ruler over the morning and evening star, present at the divine council.

facets.facet_name: Sky-Father
facets.core_claim: Zeus is the bright sky personified — the Indo-European Dyaus, the shining father. He presides over the council of the gods on Olympus.

facets.facet_name: Zeus the Tyrant (Prometheus Bound)
facets.core_claim: In Aeschylus's Prometheus Bound, Zeus is depicted as a recent tyrant who seized the throne from Kronos and punishes Prometheus for giving fire to humans. This is the Demiurge-aspect of Zeus.

facets.facet_name: Zeus Horkios (Keeper of Oaths)
facets.core_claim: Zeus enforces oaths. Those who swear falsely by Zeus are struck by his thunderbolt or pursued by the Furies.

facets.facet_name: Storm-bringer
facets.core_claim: Zeus wields the thunderbolt forged by the Cyclopes. Storm and rain are his domain.

facets.facet_name: Fire-bringer / Light-bringer
facets.core_claim: Prometheus steals fire from the gods in a fennel-stalk and gives it to humanity. For this he is chained to a rock in the Caucasus, where an eagle eats his liver each day — and it regrows each night.

facets.facet_name: All-Father
facets.core_claim: Odin is All-Father, head of the Aesir, ruler from Hliðskjálf where he sees all the worlds.

facets.facet_name: The Wanderer
facets.core_claim: Odin walks the worlds in disguise — wide-brimmed hat, blue cloak, single eye — testing kings, learning wisdom, gathering knowledge.

facets.facet_name: Seeker of Wisdom
facets.core_claim: Odin sacrifices an eye at Mimir's well to drink and gain wisdom. He learns the nine songs and seizes the mead of poetry.

facets.facet_name: The Self-Sacrificed (Hung on the World Tree)
facets.core_claim: 'I know that I hung on a windswept tree nine long nights, wounded by spear, given to Odin, myself to myself.' (Hávamál 138). He seizes the runes.

facets.facet_name: Lord of the Slain / Psychopomp
facets.core_claim: Odin gathers half the slain warriors to Valhalla, where they feast and fight and prepare for Ragnarök.

facets.facet_name: Trickster / Liberator
facets.core_claim: Loki repeatedly extracts the Aesir from disasters of their own making — and sometimes creates them. He brings them Mjölnir, Sleipnir, Gungnir, and other treasures, often through cunning theft.

facets.facet_name: The Betrayer (Killer of Baldr)
facets.core_claim: Loki contrives Baldr's death by tricking blind Höðr into throwing the mistletoe-dart. For this he is bound beneath a venom-dripping serpent.

facets.facet_name: The Bound One
facets.core_claim: Bound beneath a serpent whose venom drips on his face. At Ragnarök he breaks free and leads the forces of chaos against the gods.

facets.facet_name: The Destroyer (Cosmic Dissolver)
facets.core_claim: Shiva dissolves the manifest universe at the end of each cosmic cycle. Destruction is the precondition for renewal.

facets.facet_name: The Great Ascetic (Mahayogi)
facets.core_claim: Shiva sits in eternal meditation on Mount Kailash, ash-smeared, withdrawn from the world, the prototype of all yogis.

facets.facet_name: Dakshinamurti (The Silent Teacher)
facets.core_claim: Shiva as Dakshinamurti faces south, sits beneath a banyan tree, and transmits the highest knowledge through silence to aged sages.

facets.facet_name: The Erotic Principle (Linga / Nataraja)
facets.core_claim: Shiva as Linga is the generative principle of the cosmos. As Nataraja, he dances the universe into being and out of being simultaneously.

facets.facet_name: Bhairava (The Terrible)
facets.core_claim: Shiva in his terrible aspect, haunting cremation grounds, attended by dogs, embodying the fearsome necessity of dissolution.

facets.facet_name: Cosmic Teacher (Bhagavad Gita)
facets.core_claim: On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, Krishna reveals to Arjuna that he is the supreme Self, the source of all gods and worlds. He teaches the paths of action, devotion, and knowledge.

facets.facet_name: The Cosmic Form (Vishvarupa)
facets.core_claim: Krishna reveals to Arjuna his Vishvarupa — the cosmic form containing all beings, all worlds, all time. 'I am become Time, the destroyer of worlds.'

facets.facet_name: The Divine Lover (of Vrindavan)
facets.core_claim: Krishna of Vrindavan plays his flute and the gopis come; Radha is his eternal beloved. The lila of divine love is the deepest theology.

facets.facet_name: The Trickster Child (Butter-Thief)
facets.core_claim: Baby Krishna steals butter, mounts the hood of Kaliya the serpent, plays pranks on his mother. The trickster aspect of the supreme.

facets.facet_name: Bringer of Civilization
facets.core_claim: Quetzalcoatl descended to give humans corn, calendar, writing, the arts. He opposed blood sacrifice and was exiled by Tezcatlipoca.

facets.facet_name: Ehecatl (The Wind)
facets.core_claim: Quetzalcoatl as Ehecatl is the wind that precedes the rains, the breath of the divine in the world.

facets.facet_name: Co-Creator (with Tezcatlipoca)
facets.core_claim: Quetzalcoatl descends to Mictlan to retrieve the bones of past humanity and restores them to life with his own blood.

facets.facet_name: The Returning Prophet
facets.core_claim: Sailing east on his raft of serpents, Quetzalcoatl prophesied his return in a One Reed year.

facets.facet_name: Lord of the Night Sky
facets.core_claim: Tezcatlipoca rules the night sky and the stars. From this vantage he sees all and judges kings.

facets.facet_name: Sorcerer and Fate-Knower
facets.core_claim: Through his smoking obsidian mirror, Tezcatlipoca sees the inner thoughts of all beings and the unfolding of fate.

facets.facet_name: The Adversary / Shadow-Twin
facets.core_claim: Tezcatlipoca exiled Quetzalcoatl from Tula through trickery — sending him away with a mirror in which Quetzalcoatl saw his own face and was shamed.

facets.facet_name: King-Maker
facets.core_claim: Tezcatlipoca grants and revokes the right to rule. Tezcatlipoca-priests examined and tested new kings.

facets.facet_name: The Fallen Aeon (Mother of the Demiurge)
facets.core_claim: Sophia, last of the Aeons, attempts to create without her consort. Her error produces Yaldabaoth. She falls into the lower regions and must be recovered.

facets.facet_name: The Redeemer (Wisdom Returning)
facets.core_claim: Sophia descends to recover the divine sparks trapped in humanity. With the Christ-Aeon, she effects the return of the lost light to the Pleroma.

facets.facet_name: The Descended Maiden
facets.core_claim: Persephone is abducted by Hades and taken into the underworld. She eats the pomegranate seeds and is bound thereafter to return for part of each year. Her descent and return is the seasonal cycle made theological — and the central mystery of Eleusis.

facets.facet_name: Queen of the Underworld
facets.core_claim: Persephone reigns as queen beside Hades. The maiden becomes queen; the abducted becomes sovereign. The pattern of integration of the feared by way of becoming it.

facets.facet_name: Christ as Descended Logos
facets.core_claim: The eternal Logos — the divine Word through whom all things were made — takes on human form to teach, heal, and reveal the Father. John 1:1–14. The descended-teacher archetype in its central Western form.

facets.facet_name: The Crucifixion (Sacrificed-on-Tree)
facets.core_claim: Christ is crucified — sacrificed on the wood of the cross, pierced by the spear of Longinus, descending into death. In Trinitarian theology this is 'God offering God'; in Pauline reading, the atonement; in mystical reading, the ego dying so the true Self can rise. The supreme Western instance of the sacrificed-on-tree archetype.

facets.facet_name: The Resurrection
facets.core_claim: On the third day Christ rises. In esoteric tradition, the Harrowing of Hell: Christ descends into the underworld to free the just souls before returning. The rising-from-death motif that links Christ to every dying-rising god in the comparative cluster.

facets.facet_name: Christ Pantocrator (Cosmic Christ)
facets.core_claim: Colossians 1:15–17: 'He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation; in him all things hold together.' The Cosmic Christ encompasses all that is — structurally identical to the role Krishna assigns himself in Bhagavad Gita 11.

facets.facet_name: The Returning Christ (Parousia)
facets.core_claim: Christ will return at the end of the age — Parousia — to judge the living and the dead. The Western form of the returning-savior archetype shared with Kalki, Maitreya, the Mahdi, Saoshyant, the returning Quetzalcoatl, and the once-and-future Arthur.

facets.facet_name: The Thrice-Greatest Wisdom-Teacher
facets.core_claim: Hermes Trismegistus reveals the path of gnosis through cosmic understanding. The Emerald Tablet's 'as above, so below' is the core operating principle of the Western esoteric tradition. The synthesis of Greek Hermes and Egyptian Thoth produced the single most influential figure of Renaissance and post-Renaissance occultism.

facets.facet_name: Psychopomp — Guide of Souls
facets.core_claim: Hermes (via the Greek inheritance) is the guide of souls between worlds, the messenger who crosses every threshold. In Hermetic literature, the same function elevated: guide of the soul on its ascent through the spheres back to its source.

facets.facet_name: Shining Sky-Father (PIE root)
facets.core_claim: *Dyḗus Ph₂tḗr is the bright daytime sky personified, addressed as father. He presides over the divine council. Every Indo-European sky-father — Zeus, Jupiter, Tyr/Týr, Dyaus, Tiwaz — is a linguistically continuous descendant of this single root deity.

facets.facet_name: The Striker (Storm-Warrior root)
facets.core_claim: *Perkʷunos wields the thunder-weapon (stone axe in earlier reconstruction, hammer/thunderbolt in daughter traditions). The root from which Thor, Indra, Zeus-as-storm, Jupiter Tonans, Perun, Perkūnas, and Pērkons all derive.

facets.facet_name: Slayer of the Serpent-of-the-Waters
facets.core_claim: The PIE storm-god strikes down the serpent who has dammed up the cosmic waters, releasing the rivers and restoring cosmic order. This narrative is preserved nearly verbatim across daughter traditions: Indra vs. Vritra, Thor vs. Jörmungandr, Zeus vs. Typhon, Apollo vs. Python, Perun vs. Veles, Tarhunna vs. Illuyanka, Thraētaona vs. Aži Dahāka.

facets.facet_name: The Dawn-Maiden (PIE root)
facets.core_claim: *H₂éwsōs is the dawn personified — daughter of the sky-father, eternally young, drawn across the sky by horses. The root from which Uṣas, Ēōs, Aurōra, Aušrinė, and Ēostre all descend. The Christian feast of Easter preserves her name.

facets.facet_name: The Third Man / Cattle-Recoverer
facets.core_claim: Trito, the Third, slays the three-headed serpent who has stolen the primordial cattle and recovers the herd. This narrative — warrior recovers stolen wealth from the monster — is the founding myth of the Indo-European warrior class. Heracles vs. Geryon, Heracles vs. Cacus, Thor vs. Hrungnir, Trita vs. Viśvarūpa, Thraētaona vs. Aži Dahāka all preserve it.

facets.facet_name: Fire as Messenger Between Worlds (PIE root)
facets.core_claim: The sacrificial fire is itself divine — the visible messenger that carries the offering from human altar to heavenly recipients. Preserved most fully in Vedic Agni; the linguistic cognates (Latin ignis, Slavic ogon', Hittite Agniš) testify to the PIE root.

facets.facet_name: Cosmic Preserver
facets.core_claim: Vishnu sustains the cosmos across each yuga cycle. While Brahma creates and Shiva dissolves, Vishnu maintains. The principle of preservation in the Hindu trimurti.

facets.facet_name: Avatar Principle (Descending Savior)
facets.core_claim: Whenever dharma declines, Vishnu descends as an avatar. The Bhagavad Gita 4.7–8: 'Whenever there is a decline of dharma and a rise of adharma, then I send forth myself.' Ten classical avatars: Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, Buddha, Kalki.

facets.facet_name: Narayana — Sleeping on the Cosmic Ocean
facets.core_claim: Vishnu reclines on Shesha the cosmic serpent atop the ocean of milk, between cosmic cycles. From his navel grows the lotus from which Brahma emerges to create the next universe. In this aspect, Vishnu is the unmanifest Source from which manifestation springs.

facets.facet_name: Storm-King
facets.core_claim: Indra wields the vajra (thunderbolt) and rides the elephant Airavata. King of Svarga, the heavenly realm. The most-praised deity of the Rigveda; storm-warrior of the Indo-European root.

facets.facet_name: Slayer of Vritra
facets.core_claim: Vritra the serpent has dammed the cosmic waters and withheld them from the world. Indra, fortified by soma, strikes Vritra with the vajra and releases the waters. The Vedic preservation of the PIE storm-god-slays-water-serpent myth.

facets.facet_name: Drinker of Soma
facets.core_claim: Indra is empowered to kill Vritra only after drinking enormous quantities of soma — the sacred plant-juice of the Vedic cult, cognate with Iranian haoma. The deity who needs the ritual substance to perform his cosmic function.

facets.facet_name: Demoted Indra (Puranic / Archonic)
facets.core_claim: In the Puranic period, Indra is repeatedly humbled — by Krishna (Govardhana incident), by sages who curse him for adultery (Ahalya), by demons who occasionally seize his throne. He retains his title but loses his pre-eminence. The Vedic high-god demoted to a jealous, fallible chief-of-the-lesser-rulers — a structural parallel to the Gnostic reading of Yahweh/Yaldabaoth.

facets.facet_name: Keeper of the Oath
facets.core_claim: Varuna sees every oath sworn and binds with his noose anyone who swears falsely. The dread enforcer of sworn agreements — the Vedic parallel to Zeus Horkios, Yahweh-as-covenantal, and Mitra.

facets.facet_name: Guardian of Rta (Cosmic Order)
facets.core_claim: Varuna is the sovereign of rta — the cosmic order, truth, the way things rightly are. Cognate concept with Egyptian ma'at, Iranian asha, Chinese tao, and Greek logos/dike.

facets.facet_name: All-Seeing Sovereign
facets.core_claim: Varuna sees the flight of every bird, every wink of every eye. Originally the all-encompassing sky itself. The Vedic precursor to the omniscient sovereign of later monotheisms.

facets.facet_name: The Contract Personified
facets.core_claim: Mitra is the sworn agreement made divine. He guards alliances and punishes their breach. Paired with Varuna: Mitra is the friendly side of contract; Varuna the dread side. Invoked in the 1380 BCE Mitanni-Hittite treaty as oath-witness — one of the oldest Indo-European deity-names in any document.

facets.facet_name: Solar Mediator (Mithra/Mithras)
facets.core_claim: In the Iranian development, Mithra becomes mediator between Ahura Mazda and humanity, associated with the sun. In the late Roman Mithraic mysteries, Mithras the bull-slayer is the savior of initiates. The Indo-Iranian contract-deity transformed into a salvific figure.

facets.facet_name: Lord of the Dead, Judge of Souls
facets.core_claim: Yama receives every soul at death, weighs its deeds against the ledger kept by his scribe Chitragupta, and assigns rebirth or hell-realm accordingly. King of the southern direction.

facets.facet_name: First Mortal / First to Die
facets.core_claim: Yama is the first being who chose to die, and by dying opened the path that every subsequent being would walk. His Avestan cognate Yima (Jamshid) is preserved instead as the first king of the golden age — a fork in the Indo-Iranian myth.

facets.facet_name: Priest of the Gods (Hotr)
facets.core_claim: Agni is the divine priest who carries every offering from the human altar to the recipient god. The Rigveda opens with 'Agni I praise, the priest set forward, the divine ministrant of the sacrifice.' Fire is the mediator that makes all other Vedic rituals possible.

facets.facet_name: The Two-Headed Fire (Household & Altar)
facets.core_claim: Agni is two-headed: the household hearth that warms and cooks, and the sacrificial altar-fire that carries the offering up. Both faces are one deity. The same root divinity that becomes Vesta/Hestia in the Greco-Roman world.

facets.facet_name: The Destroyer (Mahakali)
facets.core_claim: Kali destroys what must end. She drinks the blood of Raktabija before it touches the ground (where each drop would spawn a new demon). Her destruction is not malice — it is the dissolution without which renewal cannot come.

facets.facet_name: Kali Ma — The Liberating Mother
facets.core_claim: In Tantric Shakta theology, Kali is the supreme reality itself — the formless brought into fearsome form. To devotees who can face her terror, she is the most direct path to liberation: she destroys the ego, not the soul.

facets.facet_name: Kāla — Time Personified
facets.core_claim: Kāla is time; Kālī is the feminine of time — time-as-mother. All cycles of arising and dissolving are her movement. In her fullest theology she is identical with Brahman-Nirguna in feminine voice.

facets.facet_name: The Returning Savior (End of the Kali Yuga)
facets.core_claim: At the end of the Kali Yuga, when dharma is at its lowest, Kalki will appear on a white horse, sword in hand, to destroy the kingdom of adharma and inaugurate the next Satya Yuga. The Hindu expression of the universal returning-savior archetype.

facets.facet_name: The Fallen Angels (Descenders to Hermon)
facets.core_claim: Two hundred Watchers under Shemyaza descended to Mount Hermon, swore a mutual oath to do the deed together so no single one would bear the guilt, and took human wives. For this transgression of the divine order they were bound in the abyss until the day of judgment.

facets.facet_name: Teachers of Forbidden Arts
facets.core_claim: The Watchers taught humanity arts the divine order had withheld: metallurgy (Azazel), enchantments (Shemyaza), astrology (Baraqʾel), pharmacology (Kasdeya), the cutting of roots. From the Enochic perspective, these are not gifts but corruptions. From a Promethean perspective, the parallel is exact: gods bring knowledge to humans against the high god's will, and are punished for it. The Enochic tradition frames it as transgression; Gnostic and Promethean traditions frame the same act as liberation.

facets.facet_name: Teacher of Metallurgy and Weapons
facets.core_claim: Azazel taught humans to make swords, daggers, shields, and breastplates, and taught women to adorn themselves with cosmetics and bracelets. The smith-god / weapon-teacher archetype, in its negatively-valenced Enochic form.

facets.facet_name: Bound in the Desert (Dudael)
facets.core_claim: Raphael binds Azazel hand and foot and casts him into the pit Dudael in the desert, with rough and jagged rocks piled on him. He is to remain there until the final judgment, when he will be cast into the fire. The bound-rebel-god motif.

facets.facet_name: Recipient of the Scapegoat
facets.core_claim: On Yom Kippur the high priest lays Israel's sins on the head of a goat and releases it 'to Azazel' in the wilderness. On one reading, the sins return annually to the angelic figure whose fall introduced corruption — symbolic restoration of cosmic balance via the wilderness power.

facets.facet_name: The Giants
facets.core_claim: The Nephilim are physical giants — described as so much larger than ordinary humans that Israelite spies in Canaan say 'we were like grasshoppers in our own sight, and so we were in theirs.' The category includes Rephaim, Anakim, Emim, Zamzummim, and Gibborim ('mighty ones,' Genesis 6:4).

facets.facet_name: Cause of the Flood
facets.core_claim: The Nephilim, growing beyond all measure, consumed the produce of all human labor, then consumed humans, then consumed each other. The corruption of the earth by their violence is the explicit Enochic cause of the Flood. The Flood is God's reset of a creation overrun by the offspring of the Watchers.

facets.facet_name: Spirits of the Dead Nephilim — Origin of Demons
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 15:8–11: when the Nephilim are killed in the Flood, their hybrid spirits cannot enter the rest of the dead, because they are neither fully spirit nor fully flesh. They roam the earth as 'evil spirits' — and this, in the Enochic theory, is the origin of every demon, every unclean spirit, and every possession recorded in later tradition. The Gospels' demons are, on this reading, the disembodied Nephilim still wandering.

facets.facet_name: The Ascended Seer
facets.core_claim: Enoch is taken bodily to heaven without tasting death. He tours the cosmos under angelic guidance, is shown the Watchers in their prison, intercedes (unsuccessfully) on their behalf, and is given vast astronomical and eschatological knowledge to bring back to humanity. The ascended-seer archetype.

facets.facet_name: Metatron — The Lesser YHWH
facets.core_claim: In 3 Enoch and Merkavah mysticism, Enoch is transformed into Metatron, the highest of the angels — given seventy names, enthroned at God's right hand, called 'the lesser YHWH' (yhwh ha-katan). A controversial figure in rabbinic tradition: the elevation of a human to near-divine status sits uneasily with strict monotheism. The pattern of human → divine intermediary central to Christology in Christian tradition.

facets.facet_name: Head of the Divine Council on Mount Lel
facets.core_claim: El sits on his throne at the source of the two rivers, head of the puḥru (assembly) of the gods, allotting kingship and offspring.

facets.facet_name: Creator of Creatures
facets.core_claim: El is bāniyu binwāti, 'creator of created things' — the originating father, though no longer the active world-shaper.

facets.facet_name: Grantor of Kingship
facets.core_claim: In the Legend of Kirta, El grants a son to the desperate king. He is the source of dynastic legitimacy.

facets.facet_name: Mother of the Seventy Sons of El
facets.core_claim: Asherah is consort of El, mother of the seventy sons of god — the divine council itself.

facets.facet_name: The Sacred Tree / Pole
facets.core_claim: Asherah is venerated as a sacred tree or wooden pole (the asherah-pole). Kuntillet Ajrud and Khirbet el-Qom inscriptions invoke 'YHWH and his Asherah.'

facets.facet_name: The Storm-God / Rider of Clouds
facets.core_claim: Baal rides the clouds, wields the thunder-mace, brings the rains that make the land fertile.

facets.facet_name: Slayer of Yam (Cosmic Orderer)
facets.core_claim: Baal defeats Yam (Sea / Chaos) with the mace Ayamur and is acclaimed king. The IE dragon-slaying myth in West Semitic form.

facets.facet_name: The Dying and Rising God
facets.core_claim: Baal is killed by Mot (Death), descends to the underworld, and is restored — the seasonal pattern of vegetation.

facets.facet_name: Death Personified
facets.core_claim: Mot is the gaping mouth that swallows even gods. His destruction by Anat allows the rains to return.

facets.facet_name: Prince Sea / Judge River (Primordial Chaos)
facets.core_claim: Yam claims kingship over the gods and is defeated by Baal. The cosmic-serpent/water-chaos archetype.

facets.facet_name: Warrior-Maiden and Mourner of Baal
facets.core_claim: Anat slaughters her enemies wading in blood; descends in grief and rage to find Baal; tears Mot apart with her own hands. Eros and Thanatos in one figure.

facets.facet_name: The Deified Royal Dead (rpum)
facets.core_claim: The rpum are the deified royal dead of Ugarit, summoned to ritual feasts (marziḥu) to bless the living king and the land. Their cult is centered on Mt Hermon / Bashan.

facets.facet_name: The Rephaim as Giant Warrior-Kings
facets.core_claim: In one of the two main registers, the rpum / Rephaim are the giant warrior-kings of the pre-Israelite Levant.

facets.facet_name: The Rephaim of Bashan
facets.core_claim: The Ugaritic rpum cult is geographically centered on Bashan and Mt Hermon — exactly the territory the Bible assigns to King Og (Deut 3) and exactly where 1 Enoch 6 places the descent of the Watchers. This is a single religious-geographic complex viewed from three angles.

facets.facet_name: Mother of the Tuatha Dé Danann
facets.core_claim: Danu is the ancestress of the Tuatha Dé Danann — the divine race of pre-Christian Ireland.

facets.facet_name: Goddess of the Flowing Waters
facets.core_claim: Danu is the Celtic reflex of PIE *deh₂nu, 'the flowing one' — the same goddess-name that survives as the hydronyms Danube, Don, Dnieper, Dniester, Donets, and that names the Vedic Dānu, mother of the Dānavas.

facets.facet_name: All-Father / Eochaid Ollathair
facets.core_claim: The Dagda is 'Eochaid the All-Father,' chieftain of the Tuatha Dé, possessor of the cauldron of plenty.

facets.facet_name: The Club That Kills and Revives
facets.core_claim: The Dagda's lorg mór kills with one end and revives with the other — destruction and restoration in a single instrument.

facets.facet_name: Master of All Skills (Samildánach)
facets.core_claim: Lugh declares to the Tuatha Dé that he masters every craft — when each is named he claims it, until they admit him as Samildánach.

facets.facet_name: Slayer of Balor (Dragon-Slayer Pattern)
facets.core_claim: At Mag Tuired, Lugh slings a stone through Balor's evil eye, slaying his own monstrous grandfather. The IE chaos-slayer pattern in Celtic form.

facets.facet_name: Triple Goddess of War and Fate
facets.core_claim: The Morrígan appears as raven over the battlefield, choosing who falls. Her triple form (Badb, Macha, Nemain) personifies war's many faces.

facets.facet_name: Sovereignty-Tester
facets.core_claim: The Morrígan offers herself as lover to Cú Chulainn; refused, she becomes his adversary. The sovereignty-goddess who tests the hero.

facets.facet_name: The Pre-Divine Chaos Race
facets.core_claim: The Fomorians are the older, monstrous race that the Tuatha Dé defeat at Mag Tuired to establish the cultivated order of Ireland.

facets.facet_name: Giant Chaos-Warriors
facets.core_claim: The Fomorians appear as one-eyed, one-armed, one-legged monstrous giants from beneath the sea — the chaos-form of the older order.

facets.facet_name: The Evil Eye King
facets.core_claim: Balor's single eye destroys what it looks upon — kept closed by lieutenants. His grandson Lugh slays him. The Kronos-Zeus pattern in Celtic form.

facets.facet_name: Prajāpati / Progenitor of All Races
facets.core_claim: Through his thirteen wives Kashyapa fathers the devas (via Aditi), daityas (via Diti), danavas (via Danu), nāgas (via Kadru), and many other classes of being. Every contending race shares him as common father.

facets.facet_name: Saptarṣi — Primordial Seer
facets.core_claim: Kashyapa is one of the seven primordial seers (Saptarṣi) whose vision originates the Vedas.

facets.facet_name: Mother of the Ādityas (Solar Gods)
facets.core_claim: Aditi is the mother of Varuna, Mitra, Bhaga, Indra (in some lists), Vishnu (in later layers) — the unbounded source of the bright gods.

facets.facet_name: The Unbounded (a-diti)
facets.core_claim: Aditi's name literally means 'unbounded' — the privative of 'bound.' She is the formless background against which the bounded devas appear.

facets.facet_name: Mother of the Daityas
facets.core_claim: Diti, the 'bound' counterpart to Aditi, bears the daityas. After Indra kills her sons she vows revenge; her revenge is thwarted but produces the Maruts.

facets.facet_name: Goddess of Primordial Waters
facets.core_claim: Dānu in Rigveda is the personification of the primordial waters, PIE *deh₂nu, directly cognate with Old Irish Danu, Avestan dānu, and the river-names Danube / Don / Dnieper / Dniester / Donets.

facets.facet_name: Mother of Vṛtra
facets.core_claim: RV 1.32: 'The mother of Vṛtra, Dānu, lay like a cow with her calf.' The first attested IE dragon-mother.

facets.facet_name: Mother of the Dānavas
facets.core_claim: Dānu, by Kashyapa, is the mother of the Dānavas — the asura-clan that opposes the devas.

facets.facet_name: Father of the Cosmic Mothers
facets.core_claim: Daksha gives his thirteen daughters to Kashyapa. Every class of cosmic being is therefore his grandchild — devas, asuras, daityas, danavas, nāgas, garuḍas alike.

facets.facet_name: The Defeated Older Race
facets.core_claim: The Daityas are the older, mighty divine race repeatedly defeated by the devas — the Vedic Titans.

facets.facet_name: Mighty Ascetic Asuras
facets.core_claim: Unlike Christian devils, the Daityas include moral exemplars — Prahlada the supreme devotee, Bali the just king. The asura tradition cannot be collapsed into pure evil.

facets.facet_name: The Children of Dānu (Defeated Race)
facets.core_claim: The Dānavas are descended from Dānu through Kashyapa. They are called amānuṣa ('unhuman'). Their structural parallel — and linguistic cognate — with the Celtic Tuath Dé Danann is one of the strongest goddess-cognate cases in IE comparative mythology.

facets.facet_name: Subterranean Mystic-Architects
facets.core_claim: Maya Dānava, the chief architect of the asuras, surpasses even Vishvakarma in skill. The Dānavas dwell in Bila Svarga (subterranean paradises).

facets.facet_name: The Cosmic Serpent
facets.core_claim: Vṛtra is the coiled serpent who encloses the mountains and withholds the waters. Indra's slaying of him with the vajra is the foundational IE combat-myth.

facets.facet_name: Withholder of the Cosmic Waters
facets.core_claim: Vṛtra's defining function is enclosure — he 'wraps' the waters. Slaying him releases life-giving flow. The IE dragon as obstacle-to-fertility.

facets.facet_name: Giant Warrior-Clan of Canaan
facets.core_claim: Numbers 13:33 makes the spies say of the Anakim: 'we were in our own sight as grasshoppers, and so we were in their sight.' Deuteronomy 2:11 explicitly identifies them as a sub-group of the Rephaim.

facets.facet_name: The Three Sons of Anak
facets.core_claim: Numbers 13:22 names them: Sheshai, Ahiman, Talmai — the three giant rulers of Hebron. Caleb destroys them (Joshua 15:14).

facets.facet_name: Iy-ʿAnaq in the Egyptian Execration Texts
facets.core_claim: The Egyptian Middle Kingdom Execration Texts (c. 19th–18th c. BCE) curse 'Iy-ʿanaq' — a Canaanite people whose three rulers (Erum, Abiyamimu, and another) bear Semitic names. Most scholars (Albright, Wright, Mazar) identify these as the same Anakim later confronted by Israel. Papyrus Anastasi I (13th c. BCE) describes Canaanite warriors of exceptional stature on Egyptian travel routes. This is the strongest extra-biblical historical evidence for the giant-clan tradition.

facets.facet_name: Last of the Rephaim
facets.core_claim: Deuteronomy 3:11: 'For only Og king of Bashan remained of the remnant of the Rephaim. Behold, his bedstead was a bedstead of iron... nine cubits its length, and four cubits its breadth.' Talmud Niddah 61a makes him the grandson of Shemhazai the fallen Watcher — explicitly threading him into the Genesis 6 / Enochic lineage.

facets.facet_name: Giant King at Mt Hermon / Bashan
facets.core_claim: Og's kingdom in Bashan — overlapping the Golan, Mt Hermon, and the territory of the Ugaritic rpum cult — is precisely the place where 1 Enoch 6 locates the descent of the Watchers. The three traditions converge on a single geography.

facets.facet_name: The Rephaim as Pre-Israelite Giants
facets.core_claim: Moabites called them Emim, Ammonites called them Zamzummim, Israelites called them Rephaim. They include the Anakim (Deut 2:11). Goliath and his brothers are their Philistine remnant (1 Chr 20).

facets.facet_name: Shades in Sheol
facets.core_claim: In Isaiah 14:9, Psalm 88:10, Job 26:5, the word rephaim denotes the shades of the dead. The dual sense — giants AND deified dead — is inherited directly from the Ugaritic rpum.

facets.facet_name: *Manu — The First Priest / First Man
facets.core_claim: *Manu is the first man and first priest, who establishes the cosmic order by sacrificing his twin *Yemo.

facets.facet_name: *Yemo — The Sacrificed Twin (Cosmos from Body)
facets.core_claim: *Yemo is sacrificed by *Manu; from his dismembered body the cosmos is constructed. This is the PIE prototype of cosmos-from-sacrificed-being — surviving in Norse Ymir, Vedic Puruṣa, and (via Tocharian transmission?) Chinese Pangu.

facets.facet_name: *Yemo — First King of the Dead
facets.core_claim: Because *Yemo is the first to die, he becomes the king of the dead. Reflexes: Vedic Yama, Avestan Yima (in his post-fall aspect), Norse Ymir (whose body is the world of the dead).

facets.facet_name: Cosmic Person Sacrificed at Creation
facets.core_claim: RV 10.90: the gods sacrifice Puruṣa, and from his body arise the worlds and the four varnas. The Rigvedic version of the PIE Manu/Yemo cosmogony — and the most explicit, datable instance of the cosmos-from-sacrificed-being pattern.

facets.facet_name: Puruṣa as Pure Consciousness (Sāṃkhya)
facets.core_claim: In later Sāṃkhya, Puruṣa is the pure consciousness that contemplates Prakṛti (nature/matter). The cosmogonic figure becomes a metaphysical principle.

facets.facet_name: The Cosmic Order Itself
facets.core_claim: Ṛta is not a deity but the order against which deities are measured. From PIE *h₂er- 'to fit / join properly,' it is cognate with Avestan aša (cosmic order) and conceptually parallel to Egyptian ma'at — three independent attestations of impersonal cosmic order as the highest principle.

facets.facet_name: Father in the Milky Way
facets.core_claim: Idjair dwells in the Milky Way and is the father of all Wandjinas, withdrawn from direct world-making and acting through his son Wallungunder.

facets.facet_name: Creator of Earth and First Humans (the Gyorn Gyorn)
facets.core_claim: Wallungunder, son of Idjair, created the earth and all life on it, and created the first humans — the Gyorn Gyorn (Gwion Gwion) people.

facets.facet_name: Law-Bringer to the First People
facets.core_claim: Wallungunder returned to Idjair and brought back more Wandjinas to give the Gyorn Gyorn people the Law to live by.

facets.facet_name: Rain-Maker and Fertility-Giver of the Worrorra
facets.core_claim: Namarali brings the wet-season rains, the lightning, and the cyclones that renew the land. The body covered in dots represents falling rain.

facets.facet_name: Mouthless Power — Law-Enforcer
facets.core_claim: The Wandjina has no mouth because verbal speech is unnecessary for a being whose power acts directly by will. Those who break the Law may be punished by flood, lightning, or cyclone.

facets.facet_name: Primary Wandjina of the Ngarinyin
facets.core_claim: Primary Wandjina figure of the Ngarinyin people.

facets.facet_name: Primary Wandjina of the Wunambal
facets.core_claim: Primary Wandjina figure of the Wunambal people; the Wunambal name for Wandjina generally is Gulingi.

facets.facet_name: Rainbow-Serpent Creator-Being
facets.core_claim: Wunggurr is the Kimberley reflex of the broader pan-Aboriginal Rainbow Serpent creator-being.
facets.interpretation_notes: The Rainbow Serpent parallel to the cosmic-serpent figures of Vedic / Canaanite / Mesopotamian myth is real, but distinct: the Rainbow Serpent is creative-generative rather than a chaos-being to be slain. The structural slot is different.

facets.facet_name: Lord of the Deep Waters
facets.core_claim: Wunggurr-places are deep water-holes; the Rainbow Serpent dwells in waters.

facets.facet_name: Creator and Punisher of the Beautiful Giants
facets.core_claim: The Great Spirit created an earlier race of 'beautiful giants' between the Sierra Nevada and the Rockies. When they mistreated a disfigured child the Great Spirit made the land hot and desolate and allowed enemies to conquer them. Two survived: Paiute and his wife, ancestors of the people.

facets.facet_name: Red-Haired Giant Cannibals (Later Tradition)
facets.core_claim: In later versions of the Paiute oral tradition, the Si-Te-Cah are described as red-haired giant cannibals who preyed on the Paiute and were ultimately exterminated at Lovelock Cave.

facets.facet_name: Destruction at Lovelock Cave
facets.core_claim: Allied tribes trapped the last Si-Te-Cah in Lovelock Cave, filled the entrance with brush, and set it alight, killing the survivors. The pattern of trapping-and-destroying-the-elder-race appears across many giant-traditions.

facets.facet_name: Chief of the 200 Watchers
facets.core_claim: Semyaza is the leader who proposes the descent and binds the 200 Watchers by a mutual oath on Mount Hermon (1 Enoch 6:3-6). The mountain itself is named Hermon ('curse / oath') because of this binding.

facets.facet_name: Teacher of Enchantments and Root-Cuttings
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:3: 'Semyaza taught enchantments, and root-cuttings.'

facets.facet_name: Father of Ohyah and Hahyah
facets.core_claim: Per Book of Giants 4Q530, Semyaza (Shemhazai) is father of the giant twins Ohyah and Hahyah. Talmud Niddah 61a extends the line: Shemhazai → Ahijah → Og of Bashan.

facets.facet_name: Teacher of Metallurgy, Weapons, and Cosmetics
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:1: 'And Azazel taught men to make swords, and knives, and shields, and breastplates, and made known to them the metals of the earth and the art of working them, and bracelets, and ornaments, and the use of antimony, and the beautifying of the eyelids.' The corruption of humanity by giving them technology and adornment.

facets.facet_name: Bound by Raphael in Dudael
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 10:4-8: God commands Raphael to bind Azazel hand and foot and cast him into the darkness of Dudael, where he is to remain until the day of the great judgment.

facets.facet_name: Teacher-of-Arts Bound by the Gods (Prometheus Parallel)
facets.core_claim: Azazel and Prometheus occupy the same structural slot: both give humanity transformative arts/technology, both are punished by binding. The valence is inverted: Prometheus is celebrated by Greek tradition as benefactor of humanity, Azazel is condemned by Enochic tradition as corrupter. The same act receives opposite moral judgments — one of the most interesting cases in comparative mythology.

facets.facet_name: Teacher of Earth-Signs
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:3: 'Araqiel the signs of the earth.'

facets.facet_name: Teacher of Astrology / Signs of the Stars
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:3: 'Kokabiel the constellations.' The prototype of forbidden astrological knowledge.

facets.facet_name: Teacher of Astrology / Lightning-Signs
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:3: 'Baraqiel astrology / the signs of the lightnings.'

facets.facet_name: Father of Mahaway
facets.core_claim: Per Book of Giants 4Q530 frag 7, Baraqel is father of the giant Mahaway. 4Q203 frag 8: 'Baraqel, my father, was with me.'

facets.facet_name: Teacher of Counter-Enchantments
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:3: 'Armaros the resolving of enchantments.'

facets.facet_name: Teacher of the Courses of the Moon
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:3: 'Sariel the courses of the moon.'

facets.facet_name: Teacher of the Signs of the Sun
facets.core_claim: 1 Enoch 8:3: 'Samsiel the signs of the sun.'

facets.facet_name: The Remaining Chiefs of Tens
facets.core_claim: Each commanded a unit of ten subordinate Watchers; together with the leading chiefs they account for all 200 descenders.

facets.facet_name: Throne-Room Dream of Judgment
facets.core_claim: Book of Giants 4Q530 col ii lines 15-20: Ohyah sees the Great Holy One descend, take his seat on the throne, and open the books of judgment — a vision strikingly parallel to Daniel 7:9-10.

facets.facet_name: Son of Shemhazai (the Watcher Chief)
facets.core_claim: Ohyah is a son of Shemhazai (Semyaza), the chief Watcher. Talmud Niddah 61a continues the line to Og of Bashan.

facets.facet_name: Garden-Destroyed Dream
facets.core_claim: Book of Giants 4Q530 col ii lines 6-12: Hahyah sees a great garden with many trees and roots; fire and water destroy them all except one tree of three branches — interpreted as the preservation of Noah and his three sons.

facets.facet_name: Son of Shemhazai
facets.core_claim: Hahyah is the twin of Ohyah, also son of Shemhazai.

facets.facet_name: Winged Envoy to Enoch
facets.core_claim: When the giants cannot interpret Ohyah and Hahyah's dreams, they send Mahaway to the translated Enoch. Mahaway flies on wings (Manichaean version explicit). Enoch gives him two tablets — one denouncing the giants, the second pronouncing judgment. Mahaway is the only giant who travels between earth and heaven.

facets.facet_name: Son of Baraq'el (Baraqiel)
facets.core_claim: Per Book of Giants 4Q203 frag 8: 'Baraqel, my father, was with me' — Mahaway naming his Watcher-father.

facets.facet_name: Gilgamesh among the Antediluvian Giants
facets.core_claim: Book of Giants 4Q530 col ii line 2 and 4Q531 frag 22 name Gilgamesh as one of the giants — the only known mention of Gilgamesh in pre-modern non-cuneiform literature. Direct evidence that the Mesopotamian heroic tradition was absorbed into Second Temple Jewish apocalyptic.

facets.facet_name: Hobabish — Aramaic Adaptation of Humbaba
facets.core_claim: Book of Giants 4Q203 frag 3: Hobabish's name is the Aramaic adaptation of Humbaba, the cedar-forest monster of the Gilgamesh epic.

facets.facet_name: Ahiram the Giant
facets.core_claim: A named giant in the Book of Giants; narratively less prominent than the others.

facets.facet_name: The Philistine Champion of Gath
facets.core_claim: Goliath of Gath challenges Israel for 40 days; David kills him with a sling-stone (1 Sam 17). Height given as four cubits and a span (~6'9") in 4QSamᵃ and LXX, or six cubits and a span (~9'9") in the Masoretic Text.

facets.facet_name: Descended from the Rephaim of Gath
facets.core_claim: 1 Chronicles 20:8 places Goliath and his brothers 'in the lineage of the giants' (Rephaim) of Gath — the Philistine remnant of the giant-line.

facets.facet_name: The Three Sons of Anak at Hebron
facets.core_claim: Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai are the three named sons of Anak at Hebron (Num 13:22). Caleb drives them out (Josh 15:14). Their names are Hurrian by affinity — a striking ethnographic detail that complicates the question of Anakim ethnicity.

facets.facet_name: The Four Giant-Warriors of Gath
facets.core_claim: 1 Chr 20:4-8 names four Rephaim-descended Philistine giants slain by David's men: Ishbi-benob (whose spear weighed 300 shekels of bronze), Saph / Sippai, Lahmi the brother of Goliath, and the unnamed six-fingered six-toed giant. The last remnant of the Rephaim line in Iron Age Israel.

facets.facet_name: The First Mighty Man on Earth
facets.core_claim: Genesis 10:8-12: Nimrod was 'the first on earth to be a mighty man,' 'a mighty hunter before the LORD.' Founder of Babel, Erech (Uruk), and Akkad — the Mesopotamian power centers. The first post-flood political power.

facets.facet_name: Builder of the Tower of Babel
facets.core_claim: Rabbinic tradition makes Nimrod the architect of the Tower of Babel (Gen 11) — the post-flood collective attempt to ascend to heaven, punished by the confusion of languages. Direct structural parallel to the Quinametzin who 'aspired to reach the gods' and were destroyed, and to Otus and Ephialtes piling Pelion on Ossa to reach Olympus.

facets.facet_name: The Rebel-King against YHWH
facets.core_claim: Rabbinic tradition develops Nimrod as the king who threw Abraham into the fiery furnace, the prototype of the tyrant-ruler set against the people of God. Possibly named from Hebrew mrd 'rebel.'

facets.facet_name: Giants of the Previous Sun-Era
facets.core_claim: The Quinametzin lived during a previous Sun-era (sources differ: Sun of Earth, Sun of Rain, Sun of Water). They were the dominant beings of the prior cosmic age.

facets.facet_name: Builders of Teotihuacan and Cholula
facets.core_claim: Aztec tradition credits the Quinametzin with building the Pyramid of Cholula (by Xelhua, a 20-foot giant) and the city of Teotihuacan. The 'giants built the megaliths' motif is widespread across giant-traditions globally.

facets.facet_name: Destroyed by the Gods for Their Sins
facets.core_claim: The Quinametzin were destroyed by flood, earthquake, jaguars, or hurricane (sources vary) because of their sins: aspiring to reach the gods, sins of the flesh, drunkenness, failure to venerate the gods. The structural parallel to Genesis 6 is so close that Spanish chroniclers explicitly identified the two traditions.

facets.facet_name: Their Bones Become Present-Day Humanity
facets.core_claim: Ehecatl (god of wind) takes the bones of the giants from Mictlan to create present-day humanity. Today's humans owe their reduced stature to Ehecatl having dropped and broken the giants' bones. A creation-from-the-bones-of-the-prior-race motif unique to Mesoamerica but structurally analogous to the cosmos-from-Ymir / -Tiamat / -Puruṣa pattern.

facets.facet_name: Builder of the Great Pyramid of Cholula
facets.core_claim: Xelhua, a 20-foot-tall Quinametzin, builds the Pyramid of Cholula (the largest pyramid in the world by volume) and founds seven pre-Columbian cities.

facets.facet_name: Historical 16th-Century Giant Warrior
facets.core_claim: Per Sahagún's Florentine Codex Book 12, Tzilacatzin was an Otomi warrior from Tlatelolco who stood over 10 feet tall and held off Pedro de Alvarado's Spanish forces with rocks the size of watermelons during the siege of Tenochtitlan. Rare case of a giant tradition with a named, datable, near-contemporary individual.

facets.facet_name: The Elder Defeated Race
facets.core_claim: The twelve Titans rule the world before being overthrown by the Olympians (children of Kronos) in the ten-year Titanomachy. Same structural slot as the Vedic asuras, Norse Jötnar, Irish Fomorians, biblical Nephilim/Rephaim, Aztec Quinametzin.

facets.facet_name: Imprisoned in Tartarus
facets.core_claim: After their defeat, the Titans are imprisoned in Tartarus, guarded by the Hekatonkheires. The cosmic-rebel-bound-in-pit pattern.

facets.facet_name: Children of Uranus and Gaia
facets.core_claim: The Titans are children of Uranus (Sky) and Gaia (Earth) — the IE sky-father / earth-mother primordial coupling.

facets.facet_name: Castrator of Uranus
facets.core_claim: Kronos uses the adamantine sickle given by Gaia to castrate Uranus; from the blood and severed organ are born the Erinyes, the Giants (Gigantes), and Aphrodite (from sea-foam).

facets.facet_name: Devourer of His Own Children
facets.core_claim: Kronos swallows each of his children at birth to prevent the prophecy of his overthrow. Tricked by Rhea who substitutes a stone for Zeus.

facets.facet_name: Overthrown by Zeus
facets.core_claim: Zeus, raised in secret, returns to free his siblings from Kronos's belly and leads them in the Titanomachy. Kronos is overthrown. The IE three-generation succession-pattern (Uranus → Kronos → Zeus).

facets.facet_name: Father of the Human-Related Lineage
facets.core_claim: Iapetus is father of Prometheus (who creates / champions humanity), Epimetheus, Atlas, and Menoetius. The Iapetus-Japheth name parallel is old; debated but structurally suggestive.

facets.facet_name: Bearer of the Sky
facets.core_claim: After the Titanomachy, Atlas is condemned to hold up the heavens forever. Structural parallel to the four Quinametzin sky-bearers and the Hindu Lokapalas.

facets.facet_name: Mother of the Olympians
facets.core_claim: Rhea bears the elder Olympians and saves Zeus from Kronos by substitution.

facets.facet_name: The Elder Race of Giants
facets.core_claim: The Jötnar predate the gods (Ymir is first being); the Æsir descend partly from them; they rival the Æsir continuously and destroy the world at Ragnarök.

facets.facet_name: Frost / Mountain Giants of Jötunheim
facets.core_claim: The Jötnar dwell in Jötunheim, the realm of giants. They are associated with frost, mountains, and the wild edges of the cultivated cosmos.

facets.facet_name: Destroyers of the World at Ragnarök
facets.core_claim: At Ragnarök the Jötnar, led by Surtr, cross Bifrost and destroy the cosmos with fire. The eschatological inversion of the Titanomachy — instead of being defeated by the gods, the giants finally win at the end.

facets.facet_name: Primordial Body Becomes the Cosmos
facets.core_claim: Ymir is first being. Killed by Odin, Vili, and Vé; flesh becomes earth, blood becomes sea, skull becomes sky, brain becomes clouds, eyebrows fence off Midgard. The Norse direct reflex of PIE *Yemo.

facets.facet_name: The PIE *Yemo Reflex (Linguistic Cognate)
facets.core_claim: Old Norse Ymir descends from PIE *Yemo 'twin' — direct cognate with Vedic Yama, Avestan Yima, Latin geminus. The same PIE deity-name preserved across the IE family.

facets.facet_name: Fire-Giant Who Ends the World
facets.core_claim: Surtr leads the Jötnar across Bifrost at Ragnarök and burns the cosmos with his flaming sword. The cosmic-fire-ender. Parallel to Kalki the eschatological rider.

facets.facet_name: Jötunn Mother of Odin
facets.core_claim: Bestla, a jötunn, marries Borr and bears Odin, Vili, and Vé. The Norse Æsir are therefore half-jötunn by maternal descent.

facets.facet_name: Jötunn Blood in the Æsir Line
facets.core_claim: The fact that the chief Norse god has a jötunn mother — and that Loki, who has jötunn parentage, lives among the Æsir — makes the divine-jötunn boundary porous. Structurally similar to the bnei-elohim / daughters-of-men interbreeding of Gen 6, with opposite valence (in Norse it is the norm, not the cataclysm).

facets.facet_name: Stealer of Thor's Hammer
facets.core_claim: Thrym hides Mjölnir eight miles underground and demands Freyja as bride; Thor disguises himself as Freyja and recovers the hammer, killing Thrym.

facets.facet_name: Jötunn Who Marries into the Æsir
facets.core_claim: Skadi, daughter of the jötunn Þjazi, marries Njörðr after her father's death — formalizing alliance between Æsir and jötnar.

facets.facet_name: Eschatological Horseman at the End of the Kali Yuga
facets.core_claim: Kalki, the tenth avatar of Vishnu, will appear at the end of the Kali Yuga on a white horse, with a flaming sword, to destroy the wicked and inaugurate a new age. Direct parallel to the Christian Parousia (Rev 19's rider on the white horse) and to Surtr in Norse Ragnarök.

facets.facet_name: Primordial Self-Originated Awareness
facets.core_claim: Adi-Buddha is the primordial Buddha — the ground of being from which all Buddhas and phenomena arise.

facets.facet_name: Tempter of the Buddha
facets.core_claim: Mara tries to prevent the Buddha's awakening with armies, three daughters (Desire/Discontent/Craving), and accusations. Defeated by the earth-touching gesture.

facets.facet_name: Lord of the Realm of Becoming
facets.core_claim: Mara rules the highest desire-realm heaven — the most refined samsaric trap, where conditioned happiness is mistaken for liberation.

facets.facet_name: Bodhisattva of Universal Compassion
facets.core_claim: Avalokitesvara hears all cries and comes to aid — thousand arms, thousand eyes, reaching everywhere simultaneously.

facets.facet_name: The Returning Future Buddha
facets.core_claim: Maitreya waits in Tushita heaven and will descend to re-establish dharma — the Buddhist returning-savior figure.

facets.facet_name: Swift Feminine Savior
facets.core_claim: Tara is the swift-acting feminine savior, born from Avalokitesvara's tears of compassion for all beings.

facets.facet_name: Mother of All Buddhas
facets.core_claim: Tara is 'mother of all Buddhas' — Prajnaparamita (perfect wisdom) personified as the womb from which all awakening is born.

facets.facet_name: Boundless Time / Eternity
facets.core_claim: Zurvan is Time-Eternity — the impersonal principle above and prior to both Ahura Mazda and Angra Mainyu.

facets.facet_name: Wise Lord, Creator of Good
facets.core_claim: Ahura Mazda is the supreme uncreated source of all good, light, and truth.

facets.facet_name: Source of Asha (Cosmic Truth-Order)
facets.core_claim: Ahura Mazda is the source of asha — cosmic truth-order. Cognate of Vedic rta and Egyptian Ma'at.

facets.facet_name: The Destructive Spirit
facets.core_claim: Angra Mainyu chose evil from the beginning and attacked Ahura Mazda's good creation — introducing death, disease, and falsehood.

facets.facet_name: The Cosmic Adversary (Dualistic)
facets.core_claim: Zoroastrian cosmic dualism is the oldest fully developed adversary theology — directly influencing Second Temple Judaism on Satan and evil.

facets.facet_name: The World Savior
facets.core_claim: Saoshyant will be born of a virgin from Zarathustra's preserved seed, defeat Angra Mainyu, resurrect the dead.

facets.facet_name: The Virgin-Born Savior
facets.core_claim: The Saoshyant's virgin birth is the earliest documented version of the virgin-birth savior motif — predating the Christian nativity by 600+ years.

facets.facet_name: Supreme Sovereign of Heaven
facets.core_claim: The Jade Emperor rules the celestial bureaucracy — Chinese cosmos-as-empire.

facets.facet_name: Divine Bureaucratic Administrator
